sentences.text: When we were in Wieliczka, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- my husband was there, and I was there.
Now, at that time, we were not married.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we knew each other from the ghetto.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we worked at-- as I said, I worked carrying furniture and washing dishes and whatnot, and sometimes I had a turn in the kitchen.
And when I was in the kitchen, the butcher was bringing the meat.
And he was friendly, so I was friendly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day-- but I can't really remember whether we were there three months, four months, two months-- I can't remember.
One day, he came and he said, you know, the word is going around that they're going to finish you off-- from here take you to somewhere where they finish off people.
I didn't really know what he meant by finishing off people.
But he said, I don't know either.
But there is a camp somewhere, where the people go in and never come out.
So I said, why are you telling me?
And I said, well, what can I do about it?
And he said, look, Christmas is coming up.
Christmas Eve, we have Christmas dinner with guests.
We'll have music, make loud the music.
But you have to make your way, very quietly, to my place-- which he described to me, how I have to go-- and go into the barn.
I must mention, I was petrified of animals, maybe more than meeting a Nazi on the way, because I will drown in the snow and he couldn't probably see me.
But an animal together with me in the barn, that was something I couldn't visualize.
But life was at stake.
So we were walking, and walking, and walking with my husband, and to find the way wasn't easy, because it was all covered with snow.
I fell into a ditch.
He couldn't find me.
It was-- we came all right there, and they was just singing the Christmas carols and whatnot.
And we sneaked in, into the barn, which I presumed was the barn.
Anyhow, as he said, he will bring us some food, which he did.
And then he sneaked in, while the Germans were drunk.
They got drunk like a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the Poles could drink, too-- very well, thank you.
So he sneaked in and said, they are all drunk.
They will sleep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll come here at 4:00 in the morning, and I'll collect you.
I'll have to put you under-- between the carcasses of the frozen meat.
And I'll take you to the ghetto-- just to the gate, and the rest is up to you.
So that was like a dream, [INAUDIBLE] like an angel from heaven.
I always thought that was my father who was watching.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not that I knew that my father was dead, by then, but-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] anyhow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We made it to the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to walk from-- this was risky, because we didn't have the Star of David.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You knew, more or less, which Polish policeman-- and my husband was always-- also working for the underground, simultaneously, from the ghetto.
So he knew some policemen who were in the underground, too.
So we had to wait a little bit, until-- and we got back into the ghetto.
And for me, unfortunately, I didn't have anymore where to go back to, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they have liquidated the part of the ghetto where I lived.
And my sister was taken away to Auschwitz.
How I know that she went to Auschwitz, because a person that has survived saw her going into-- with the transport into the chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came back and I stood in the snow.
Didn't have where to go.
So Peter suggested [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we-- that he will go up and see his parents, and tell them that he will bring me up there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I went up.
I cried, obviously.
And his mother asked me who I am, who he picked up.
I said who I am, and she said, what a coincidence.
I was sitting in the synagogue next to your mother.
What a lovely person she was, and all that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very nice to me.
And he said-- Peter said-- I want to marry her now.
And I even whispered to his ear, what's the urgency?
Doesn't look very nice.
And he said, never mind what happened to us.
Let-- if anything happens, then at least let's be together.
And so we went out to the rabbi in the same building, on the ground floor-- Rabbi Shapiro, Spiro, some-- Spira?
No, Shapiro, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
10 Men from the building [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there wasn't any wine, just powdered milk to say a [INAUDIBLE].
And that was it.
Pardon?
[INAUDIBLE].
No.
A night before-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where did I-- yeah, that's right, that night.
We didn't know where we are going.
But the ghetto was divided into ghetto A and ghetto B.
Ghetto B was empty, because they had taken away all the Jews.
And that's a separate, long story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The part where I lived has become ghetto B.
So I couldn't go to that part-- or was it ghetto B?
I can't remember, now.
But it certainly was in a close proximity.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And his mother-- my-- Peter, my husband's mother-- gave us a [INAUDIBLE].
And they were just giving out the rations, the bread you could pick up.
So I had my card and he had his.
So imagine how rich we were.
We got a whole loaf of bread.
Was boiling hot-- this bread, I mean.
I can't imagine it, eating now, hot bread.
But I didn't get a sore stomach then, but I have it now.
Well, anyhow, we walked into the first place.
It was dark, no light.
We just walked in.
Hello?
Anybody here?
And we just walked into a room, and there was just a bit of light from the moon.
And there was a big round table-- nowhere to put the [INAUDIBLE], put it on the floor.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sitting on one side of the table.
He was sitting on the other.
We ripped the hot bread apart.
What was important is to eat the bread.
Kissing and hugging, well, as long as you knew that you are not alone, the rest wasn't really important.
Not that I knew much about it, anyway.
That's by the by.
So that was in ghetto.
But then, of course, the liquidation of the ghetto came.
And with his mother and with thousands of other Jews, we were marching to Plaszow.
Who couldn't keep up was shot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
It was a tiring walk.
His mother wasn't a very well person, health-wise.
She was a diabetic, and she couldn't get what she needed.
And I almost had to carry her, with all my strengths.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got there--
Pardon?
Only what you could put in a pocket.
Nothing else.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when we came to Plaszow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, the Germans came.
Anything you had-- a watch, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a ring, a wedding ring-- my wedding ring was made of tin, like maybe from a Coca-Cola tin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not that we had Coca-Cola, it was something else.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But you-- this ring could-- if you had a golden ring, you would rather buy food, because you were forever hungry.
So that wasn't important at all.
To Zablocie.
We went to Zablocie, and as I said, first, we were just escorted to Zablocie and back to the camp.
And then Schindler's decided to build a camp, and we were there-- taken there permanently.
In-- at Schindler's camp?
Kitchen cleaning.
There wasn't-- and in the factory, I mean, he was producing pots and pans.
Not that I know a thing about it, because he didn't expect us, really, to produce much.
Mr.
Bunkier ?] knew how to run the business, and it was all gone under the name of the [GERMAN], you know?
Like he was managing.
Schindler didn't know much about pots and pans.
No.
That's right.
But he was such a delightful man.
He was just a simply delightful man.
Approachable, very easily approachable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would stop with you, and joke, and, what's your name again?
I said-- we used to say, we are number.
Not to me, you are not.
And you know, he was very easy to-- if you had problems, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, say, you were not well or something, you could tell him that.
And he was a very understanding person.
But of course, we didn't know what he has got on his mind.
We were happy there.
We were not overeating, mind you-- still the rations.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was better than Plaszow.
And suddenly, we were told we have to go back to Plaszow.
That's when it became the [GERMAN] camp, Lager.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Schindler told us that he would see us in Czechoslovakia.
And we cried bitter tears, because we didn't believe that you can just come and take us out, and take us where he wants us to take us.
We just couldn't believe it.
So, it was back to Plaszow, to the routine, to the bookbindery, and that was it.
Until one day, they were taking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people from Plaszow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] men first, calling out and putting in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cattle trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they called out women, 300 women.
My name wasn't on it, and I was terribly disappointed.
There were a few women which should have been there.
Their names were not called out, but there were names of other women who were never there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I immediately knew what has happened.
One of the influential-- or two of the influential-- people among us, together with a German, of course, have crossed us off the list, and put their mistresses on the list.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because that-- I could not-- we couldn't understand what happened.
We knew that we have to go to Brunnlitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the men have gone.
We have gone back to the barracks.
And the 300 women were standing there, and I-- I was absolutely shattered to pieces that I wasn't called out.
And there were other very nice girls that-- we just couldn't work.
And they took us back to our compounds, there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was an SS woman who was coming every morning to check how we arranged our blankets and everything.
It was like in an army.
If it-- if it was that much out of-- sticking out, or uneven, or whatever, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she would beat you to-- until you dropped.
But she fancied me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not that I knew, at that time.
They were saying she's lesbian.
I know now what a lesbian is, but I didn't know then.
So she-- because they cut our hair very short in Plaszow, just almost like a boy.
So I thought, well, what's a lesbian?
Nobody knew-- I mean, girls-- young girls these days-- in those days didn't know these-- the terminology, or that something like that exists.
Nobody talked about it.
Not-- you know.
So she somehow fancied me.
And I said-- she saw-- she came and saw that I'm crying.
And she says, why are you crying?
But she had a voice like a man, and was tall like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I said, I can't understand it.
There's a mistake somewhere.
But then, with the girls, we knew what happened.
We put two and two together, you know?
We were in the [GERMAN], and we were not called out to Brunnlitz.
Mr.
Schindler will be terribly upset.
And she said, they haven't gone.
And I said, they haven't gone?
Why?
Not enough trains.
I said, when will they go?
And she said, in two or three days.
Oh, that gave me-- that was food for my thought.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And next morning-- this I'll never forget, never ever.
Next morning, I got up.
We were called up.
I mean, you know, we had to get up, in a hurry, always.
And on this day, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how was I still in the barrack?
I can't remember.
But I remember telling that girl that was supposed to be on that list with me-- don't forget, there were 800 women to one barrack.
I said listen, it's pouring outside-- absolutely pouring.
I don't think-- you remember the big storm we had here, not so long ago?
That's what it was.
We were here.
There was the roll call Platz here, and the German officers here.
And I said, look.
It's pouring outside.
It-- there isn't a fly outside.
I am going to see Schubke.
Schubke was the German SS man [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was sitting in these quarters, there.
And she said, you are mad.
And I said, probably.
But I'll try.
And she said, no.
They will shoot-- shoot me.
I haven't-- we haven't got passes.
And I said, well, he come out of the sentry, from-- it's nice and warm inside.
He will come out.
And I said, look, I haven't got much time.
Are you coming or not?
And she said, you are out of your mind.
I hope I see you back.
And I said, well, see you.
And I was running through that Platz, I tell you, as though some-- something was pushing me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the sentry knocked-- when I ran through-- ran through there-- she knocked in the window.
Where is the pass?
And I said-- here.
Well, if you say something, I can't open it.
It's raining.
It will pull apart.
So he thought that I have it in my fist.
I had nothing there.
That was fine.
I walked in, and I saw Schubke on the door-- the name.
I walked in.
To my disappointment, Miller was sitting here.
He was the [GERMAN], which means he dealt with the employment.
He was a very dangerous man.
Schubke wasn't, but he was an extremely dangerous man.
And even more dangerous was his dog.
If they wanted, they sent a dog at you, and you were chewed up to pieces.
I nearly died when I walked in.
I knocked on the door, of course.
And when they said [GERMAN], so Schubke said, who are you?
What do you want?
I said, I am [GERMAN], which is a prisoner.
I just wanted to draw your attention to the fact that a big mistake has occurred-- me and the mistakes, all the time.
And he said, what mistake?
And I said, look, I'm-- I was working at the [GERMAN], and somehow I'm not on the list.
You mean to tell me that you didn't have enough money for Goldberg and company?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said-- I couldn't say no, I didn't have the money, because he was in it, too.
So I said, money?
We don't have money here.
We are not allowed to have money.
It's just a simple mistake.
And he said, you're sure you were working for Schindler?
And I said, of course I'm sure.
I wouldn't have come, otherwise.
Miller, you go and check.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you know what waits for you if you are lying.
And I said, I am quite aware of it.
And if I would be lying, I would deserve what-- what's waiting for me.
So I was standing there, hoping for the best, pray-- talking to my mother, help me, [INAUDIBLE], help me.
I mean in my mind.
And it took a while.
And he said-- he came back, and he said, yes, she was in the [GERMAN].
So Schubke said, I'll see to it-- he made a note-- I'll see to it that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you are on the list.
And who is-- who was in your place?
And I said, I wouldn't know.
How would I know?
Anyhow, to get out was even more frightening, because I was afraid that, as I go out, he will send the dog at me.
So I didn't turn with my back to them.
I was just coming with my back towards the door.
I said danke schoen, danke schoen a million times.
And when I left the room, his office, and I was going down the stairs, towards the stairs, I saw a woman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said to myself, couldn't be true.
At the same time, my mind was working like-- it was split in two.
How do I get out, now?
I hope it's still pouring.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But who is that woman?
As we came closer to each other, she said, Syda?
And I said, Mimi!
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my cousin's wife.
She was a Viennese-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 100% Aryan type, so she wanted to live on the-- I gave you the list, and she's there.
And they caught her, and brought her to Plaszow.
But because she spoke German, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was the typist in the office, in a [GERMAN] administration office.
So she-- not-- she wasn't typing the confidential stuff, only the everyday.
She says, what are you doing?
So I told her, quickly.
And I said, do you think he will would put me on the list?
She said, well, he usually-- in a case like that, he asks me if I know someone that I care for.
So if he does-- and she-- he did, and that was just pure lucky.
And I said, Mimi, how do I get out of here?
[INAUDIBLE] It's not pouring like it did.
So she scribbled and gave an SS man to sign it-- a woman or whoever.
I don't know.
I didn't follow her.
But I was frightened stiff, I can tell you.
We'll break there, for the end of the tape.
Pardon?
